title: Encounter Building
summary: Guidelines for creating balanced combat encounters using XP budgets and challenge rating, including thresholds by character level and encounter multipliers.
content:
## Encounter Building

Building balanced encounters is one of the most important DM skills. The core system uses XP budgets based on character level and encounter difficulty.

### XP Thresholds by Character Level

Each character has XP thresholds for four difficulty levels. To build an encounter, multiply the threshold by the number of characters.

| Level | Easy | Medium | Hard | Deadly |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 25 | 50 | 75 | 100 |
| 2 | 50 | 100 | 150 | 200 |
| 3 | 75 | 150 | 225 | 400 |
| 4 | 125 | 250 | 375 | 500 |
| 5 | 250 | 500 | 750 | 1,100 |
| 6 | 300 | 600 | 900 | 1,400 |
| 7 | 350 | 750 | 1,100 | 1,700 |
| 8 | 450 | 900 | 1,400 | 2,100 |
| 9 | 550 | 1,100 | 1,600 | 2,400 |
| 10 | 600 | 1,200 | 1,900 | 2,800 |
| 11 | 800 | 1,600 | 2,400 | 3,600 |
| 12 | 1,000 | 2,000 | 3,000 | 4,500 |
| 13 | 1,100 | 2,200 | 3,400 | 5,100 |
| 14 | 1,250 | 2,500 | 3,800 | 5,700 |
| 15 | 1,400 | 2,800 | 4,300 | 6,400 |
| 16 | 1,600 | 3,200 | 4,800 | 7,200 |
| 17 | 2,000 | 3,900 | 5,900 | 8,800 |
| 18 | 2,100 | 4,200 | 6,300 | 9,500 |
| 19 | 2,400 | 4,900 | 7,300 | 10,900 |
| 20 | 2,800 | 5,700 | 8,500 | 12,700 |

### How to Calculate an Encounter Budget

1. **Determine party thresholds.** For each PC, look up their level on the table above. Add all PCs' thresholds together for each difficulty tier.
   - Example: Four level 5 characters have a Medium threshold of 4 × 500 = 2,000 XP.
2. **Add up monster XP.** Total the XP values of all monsters in the encounter.
3. **Apply the encounter multiplier** (see below) based on the number of monsters.
4. **Compare.** The adjusted XP determines the encounter difficulty.

### Encounter Multipliers

Multiple monsters are more dangerous than their raw XP suggests. Multiply total XP by:

| Number of Monsters | Multiplier |
|---|---|
| 1 | ×1 |
| 2 | ×1.5 |
| 3–6 | ×2 |
| 7–10 | ×2.5 |
| 11–14 | ×3 |
| 15+ | ×4 |

**Important:** The multiplier is only for determining difficulty. Award the actual (unmultiplied) XP to the party.

**Party size adjustments:**
- Fewer than 3 PCs: Use the next higher multiplier.
- More than 5 PCs: Use the next lower multiplier.

### Encounter Difficulty Descriptions

- **Easy:** Little to no risk of death. Resources barely taxed. Good for warm-up or narrative encounters.
- **Medium:** A moderate challenge. One or two scary moments, but the party should prevail. Good baseline difficulty.
- **Hard:** A serious fight. Weaker characters might drop to 0 HP. The party will need to use resources wisely.
- **Deadly:** Potentially lethal. One or more characters may die. Use sparingly, usually for climactic battles.

### Adventuring Day XP Budget

The adventuring day XP budget tells you roughly how much total encounter XP a party can handle between long rests.

| Level | Adjusted XP Per Day Per Character |
|---|---|
| 1 | 300 |
| 2 | 600 |
| 3 | 1,200 |
| 4 | 1,700 |
| 5 | 3,500 |
| 6 | 4,000 |
| 7 | 5,000 |
| 8 | 6,000 |
| 9 | 7,500 |
| 10 | 9,000 |
| 11 | 10,500 |
| 12 | 11,500 |
| 13 | 13,500 |
| 14 | 15,000 |
| 15 | 18,000 |
| 16 | 20,000 |
| 17 | 25,000 |
| 18 | 27,000 |
| 19 | 30,000 |
| 20 | 40,000 |

Multiply by the number of PCs. Spread this across 6–8 encounters with 1–2 short rests.

### Practical Tips

- **The math is a starting point, not gospel.** Party composition, magic items, and player skill all affect actual difficulty.
- **Action economy matters most.** Five weak monsters can be more dangerous than one strong monster because they get more attacks per round.
- **Terrain and tactics** can shift an encounter up or down a full difficulty tier.
- **Don't be afraid to adjust mid-combat.** Add reinforcements, have monsters flee, or reduce hit points behind the screen.
- **Waves** are a great tool: start with a manageable fight, then add a second wave if the party is handling it easily.

title: Challenge Rating Explained
summary: A comprehensive explanation of what Challenge Rating means, how it relates to party level, and how to use it when selecting monsters for encounters.
content:
## Challenge Rating Explained

Challenge Rating (CR) is a monster's overall power rating, designed to help DMs gauge how dangerous a creature is.

### What CR Means

A creature's CR indicates the level of a party of four adventurers for which that creature would be a **medium-difficulty** encounter. A CR 5 monster is a medium challenge for four level-5 characters.

### CR vs. Party Level (Rules of Thumb)

- **CR = Party Level:** A single monster is a medium encounter for four characters.
- **CR = Party Level + 2 to 3:** A single monster is a hard or deadly encounter.
- **CR = Party Level – 2 or lower:** Trivial as a solo monster, but useful in groups.
- **CR = 2 × Party Level:** Extremely deadly; likely a TPK without very good tactics or magic items.

### Proficiency Bonus by CR

A monster's proficiency bonus is determined by its CR:

| CR | Proficiency Bonus |
|---|---|
| 0 | +2 |
| 1/8 | +2 |
| 1/4 | +2 |
| 1/2 | +2 |
| 1–4 | +2 |
| 5–8 | +3 |
| 9–12 | +4 |
| 13–16 | +5 |
| 17–20 | +6 |
| 21–24 | +7 |
| 25–28 | +8 |
| 29–30 | +9 |

### Monster Statistics by CR

Expected ranges for a monster of a given CR:

| CR | AC | HP | Attack Bonus | Damage/Round | Save DC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | ≤13 | 1–6 | ≤+3 | 0–1 | ≤13 |
| 1/4 | 13 | 36–49 | +3 | 2–3 | 13 |
| 1 | 13 | 71–85 | +3 | 9–14 | 13 |
| 5 | 15 | 131–145 | +6 | 33–38 | 15 |
| 10 | 17 | 206–220 | +7 | 63–68 | 16 |
| 15 | 18 | 281–295 | +8 | 93–98 | 18 |
| 20 | 19 | 356–400 | +10 | 123–140 | 19 |
| 25 | 19 | 446–475 | +12 | 165–175 | 21 |
| 30 | 19 | 546–600 | +14 | 201–220 | 23 |

### Common CR Misconceptions

- **"A CR 1 monster should be easy for a level 1 party."** Not quite — a CR 1 monster is a *medium* challenge for four level-1 characters. A solo CR 1 creature can absolutely kill a single level-1 PC.
- **"Higher CR always means more dangerous."** Not in practice. A CR 5 monster with all melee attacks in an open field is less dangerous than a CR 3 caster with *Fireball* and good positioning.
- **"CR accounts for everything."** It doesn't factor in terrain, tactics, party composition, or magic items. Use it as a guideline, not a guarantee.

### Fractional CR

Monsters below CR 1 use fractional ratings:
- **CR 0:** Essentially harmless (commoners, small animals). 0 or 10 XP.
- **CR 1/8:** Minor threats (bandits, guard dogs). 25 XP.
- **CR 1/4:** Low-level threats (goblins, skeletons). 50 XP.
- **CR 1/2:** Moderate threats for level 1 (orcs, hobgoblins). 100 XP.

title: Experience Points
summary: XP awards by challenge rating, the milestone leveling alternative, and character advancement thresholds from level 1 to level 20.
content:
## Experience Points

Experience points (XP) measure a character's progress toward the next level. XP can be awarded for defeating monsters, completing quests, and overcoming challenges.

### XP by Challenge Rating

| CR | XP |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 or 10 |
| 1/8 | 25 |
| 1/4 | 50 |
| 1/2 | 100 |
| 1 | 200 |
| 2 | 450 |
| 3 | 700 |
| 4 | 1,100 |
| 5 | 1,800 |
| 6 | 2,300 |
| 7 | 2,900 |
| 8 | 3,900 |
| 9 | 5,000 |
| 10 | 5,900 |
| 11 | 7,200 |
| 12 | 8,400 |
| 13 | 10,000 |
| 14 | 11,500 |
| 15 | 13,000 |
| 16 | 15,000 |
| 17 | 18,000 |
| 18 | 20,000 |
| 19 | 22,000 |
| 20 | 25,000 |
| 21 | 33,000 |
| 22 | 41,000 |
| 23 | 50,000 |
| 24 | 62,000 |
| 25 | 75,000 |
| 26 | 90,000 |
| 27 | 105,000 |
| 28 | 120,000 |
| 29 | 135,000 |
| 30 | 155,000 |

### Character Advancement (XP Thresholds)

| Level | XP Required | Total XP to Reach |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 300 | 300 |
| 3 | 600 | 900 |
| 4 | 1,800 | 2,700 |
| 5 | 3,800 | 6,500 |
| 6 | 7,500 | 14,000 |
| 7 | 9,000 | 23,000 |
| 8 | 11,000 | 34,000 |
| 9 | 14,000 | 48,000 |
| 10 | 16,000 | 64,000 |
| 11 | 21,000 | 85,000 |
| 12 | 15,000 | 100,000 |
| 13 | 20,000 | 120,000 |
| 14 | 20,000 | 140,000 |
| 15 | 25,000 | 165,000 |
| 16 | 30,000 | 195,000 |
| 17 | 30,000 | 225,000 |
| 18 | 40,000 | 265,000 |
| 19 | 40,000 | 305,000 |
| 20 | 50,000 | 355,000 |

### Dividing XP

When monsters are defeated, divide the total XP equally among all party members who participated. XP is divided, not multiplied — a CR 5 monster gives 1,800 XP total, or 450 XP to each member of a four-person party.

### Non-Combat XP

The DMG suggests awarding XP for non-combat challenges:
- **Traps and hazards:** Award XP as if the trap were a monster of equivalent difficulty.
- **Social encounters:** If a social challenge was as difficult as a combat encounter, award equivalent XP.
- **Exploration and puzzles:** Award XP based on difficulty and significance.

### Milestone Leveling (Alternative)

Many DMs prefer milestone leveling over tracking XP:

**How It Works:** Characters level up when they reach significant story milestones — completing a quest, defeating a major villain, discovering a crucial secret, or finishing a story arc.

**Advantages:**
- No bookkeeping or XP tracking.
- The DM controls pacing — no need to worry about the party being over- or under-leveled.
- Encourages roleplaying and quest completion over "grinding" monsters for XP.
- Players can't game the system by seeking unnecessary fights.

**Disadvantages:**
- Players lose the tangible sense of progress between levels.
- Requires the DM to judge when leveling is appropriate.
- Can feel arbitrary if not communicated clearly.

**Tips for Milestone Leveling:**
- Tell players upfront that you're using milestones.
- Give a rough sense of when levels will happen ("you'll probably level up after this dungeon").
- Level up at dramatically satisfying moments — after the boss fight, not during a long rest three days later.
- Generally aim for a level every 2–3 sessions at low levels, 3–5 sessions at higher levels.

title: Random Encounters
summary: How to build and use random encounter tables by environment, including frequency, pacing, and making random encounters feel meaningful.
content:
## Random Encounters

Random encounters add unpredictability, reinforce the danger of travel, and bring the world to life. A well-designed random encounter table reflects the ecology and dangers of a region.

### When to Use Random Encounters

- **Overland travel** through dangerous territory.
- **Dungeon exploration** over extended periods.
- **Wilderness survival** campaigns where danger is constant.
- **City patrol** or downtime in a dangerous neighborhood.

### How Often to Check

A common approach: roll a d20 at regular intervals.

| Environment | Check Frequency | Encounter On |
|---|---|---|
| Safe roads | Once per day | 18–20 |
| Wilderness | Three times per day (morning, afternoon, night) | 17–20 |
| Dangerous wilderness | Three times per day | 15–20 |
| Dungeon | Every 30 minutes of game time, or when the party is noisy | 18–20 |
| Haunted/Cursed area | Hourly | 15–20 |

### Building a Random Encounter Table

**Step 1: Choose the Environment**

Each table should be tailored to a specific environment and tier of play:
- Arctic, Coastal, Desert, Forest, Grassland, Hill, Mountain, Swamp, Underdark, Underwater, Urban

**Step 2: Populate the Table**

Use a d12 or d20 table. Weight results so common encounters are more likely:

| d12 | Result Type |
|---|---|
| 1 | Dangerous encounter (above party level) |
| 2–3 | Hard encounter |
| 4–6 | Medium encounter |
| 7–8 | Easy encounter |
| 9–10 | Environmental hazard or obstacle |
| 11 | NPC or social encounter |
| 12 | Discovery (ruins, treasure, lore) |

**Step 3: Fill In Specifics**

Example — Forest (Levels 1–4):

| d12 | Encounter |
|---|---|
| 1 | 1 owlbear |
| 2 | 1d4 dire wolves |
| 3 | 2d4 wolves |
| 4 | 1d6 goblins with a goblin boss |
| 5 | 1d4 giant spiders |
| 6 | 2d4 bandits |
| 7 | 1d4 pixies (non-hostile) |
| 8 | 1 druid tending a wounded animal |
| 9 | Quicksand or hidden pit trap |
| 10 | An ancient, overgrown shrine |
| 11 | A traveling merchant with 2 guards |
| 12 | A hidden grove with a healing spring (restores 2d4 HP) |

### Encounters by Environment

**Arctic:** Polar bears, winter wolves, yetis, frost giants, ice mephits, blizzards, thin ice.

**Coastal:** Sahuagin, merfolk, sea hags, giant crabs, pirates, shipwrecks, tidal surges.

**Desert:** Giant scorpions, gnolls, mummies, blue dragons, dust devils, mirages, oases.

**Forest:** Wolves, owlbears, treants, dryads, bandits, elven patrols, ruins, will-o'-wisps.

**Grassland:** Lions, gnolls, centaurs, tribal warriors, stampedes, wildfires.

**Hill:** Orcs, ogres, hill giants, griffons, wyverns, landslides.

**Mountain:** Rocs, stone giants, harpies, galeb duhr, avalanches, precarious bridges.

**Swamp:** Lizardfolk, black dragons, shambling mounds, bullywugs, will-o'-wisps, quicksand.

**Underdark:** Drow patrols, mind flayers, hook horrors, ropers, fungi fields, cave-ins.

**Urban:** Thieves, cultists, corrupt guards, street performers hiding something, mysterious strangers.

### Making Random Encounters Meaningful

- **Not every encounter needs to be combat.** Include NPCs, discoveries, environmental challenges, and clues.
- **Connect encounters to the story.** A random bandit encounter is forgettable; bandits wearing the symbol of the BBEG's organization is a story hook.
- **Use encounters to show the world.** A random encounter with refugees fleeing a dragon shows the dragon's impact better than exposition.
- **Foreshadowing.** Finding giant claw marks on trees foreshadows the dragon encounter later.
- **Resource drain.** Random encounters serve the mechanical purpose of draining party resources before the "real" fight.

### Frequency and Pacing

- Too many random encounters slow the game and feel tedious.
- Too few make travel feel safe and boring.
- **Aim for 1–2 random encounters per day of travel.** Skip or narrate past uneventful days.
- **Let players avoid encounters.** If scouts spot danger ahead, let the party choose to engage or circumvent.

title: Treasure and Loot
summary: Individual treasure tables, treasure hoard tables by CR, art objects, gemstones, and guidelines for distributing magic items and wealth.
content:
## Treasure and Loot

Treasure is one of the primary rewards in D&D. The DMG provides tables for generating random treasure carried by individual monsters and stored in treasure hoards.

### Individual Treasure

Roll on the appropriate table when a creature is carrying treasure on its person.

**Individual Treasure: CR 0–4**

| d100 | Treasure |
|---|---|
| 01–30 | 5d6 (17) cp |
| 31–60 | 4d6 (14) sp |
| 61–70 | 3d6 (10) ep |
| 71–95 | 3d6 (10) gp |
| 96–100 | 1d6 (3) pp |

**Individual Treasure: CR 5–10**

| d100 | Treasure |
|---|---|
| 01–30 | 4d6 × 100 (1,400) cp, 1d6 × 10 (35) ep |
| 31–60 | 6d6 × 10 (210) sp, 2d6 × 10 (70) gp |
| 61–70 | 3d6 × 10 (105) ep, 2d6 × 10 (70) gp |
| 71–95 | 4d6 × 10 (140) gp |
| 96–100 | 2d6 × 10 (70) gp, 3d6 (10) pp |

**Individual Treasure: CR 11–16**

| d100 | Treasure |
|---|---|
| 01–20 | 4d6 × 100 (1,400) sp, 1d6 × 100 (350) gp |
| 21–35 | 1d6 × 100 (350) ep, 1d6 × 100 (350) gp |
| 36–75 | 2d6 × 100 (700) gp, 1d6 × 10 (35) pp |
| 76–100 | 2d6 × 100 (700) gp, 2d6 × 10 (70) pp |

**Individual Treasure: CR 17+**

| d100 | Treasure |
|---|---|
| 01–15 | 2d6 × 1,000 (7,000) ep, 8d6 × 100 (2,800) gp |
| 16–55 | 1d6 × 1,000 (3,500) gp, 1d6 × 100 (350) pp |
| 56–100 | 1d6 × 1,000 (3,500) gp, 2d6 × 100 (700) pp |

### Treasure Hoards

Hoards represent the accumulated wealth of a lair or dungeon. They include coins, gems, art objects, and magic items.

**Treasure Hoard: CR 0–4**
- Coins: 6d6 × 100 cp, 3d6 × 100 sp, 2d6 × 10 gp
- Plus roll d100 for gems/art and magic items (see tables below)

**Treasure Hoard: CR 5–10**
- Coins: 2d6 × 100 cp, 2d6 × 1,000 sp, 6d6 × 100 gp, 3d6 × 10 pp
- Plus roll d100 for gems/art and magic items

**Treasure Hoard: CR 11–16**
- Coins: 4d6 × 1,000 gp, 5d6 × 100 pp
- Plus roll d100 for gems/art and magic items

**Treasure Hoard: CR 17+**
- Coins: 12d6 × 1,000 gp, 8d6 × 1,000 pp
- Plus roll d100 for gems/art and magic items

### Gemstones

**10 gp Gems:** Azurite, banded agate, blue quartz, eye agate, hematite, lapis lazuli, malachite, moss agate, obsidian, rhodochrosite, tiger eye, turquoise.

**50 gp Gems:** Bloodstone, carnelian, chalcedony, chrysoprase, citrine, jasper, moonstone, onyx, quartz, sardonyx, star rose quartz, zircon.

**100 gp Gems:** Amber, amethyst, chrysoberyl, coral, garnet, jade, jet, pearl, spinel, tourmaline.

**500 gp Gems:** Alexandrite, aquamarine, black pearl, blue spinel, peridot, topaz.

**1,000 gp Gems:** Black opal, blue sapphire, emerald, fire opal, opal, star ruby, star sapphire, yellow sapphire.

**5,000 gp Gems:** Black sapphire, diamond, jacinth, ruby.

### Art Objects

**25 gp Art Objects:** Silver ewer, carved bone statuette, small gold bracelet, cloth-of-gold vestments, black velvet mask, copper chalice, pair of bone dice.

**250 gp Art Objects:** Gold ring with bloodstones, carved ivory statuette, gold bracelet, silk robe with silver thread, large well-made tapestry, brass mug with jade inlay, box of turquoise animal figurines.

**750 gp Art Objects:** Silver chalice with moonstones, silver-plated steel longsword with jet gems, carved harp of exotic wood with ivory inlay, small gold idol, gold dragon comb with red garnets.

**2,500 gp Art Objects:** Fine gold chain with fire opal, old masterpiece painting, embroidered silk and velvet mantle with moonstones, platinum bracelet with sapphire, embroidered glove with jewel chips.

**7,500 gp Art Objects:** Jeweled gold crown, jeweled platinum ring, small gold statuette set with rubies, gold cup set with emeralds, gold jewelry box with platinum filigree.

### Magic Item Distribution by Rarity and Level

| Character Level | Appropriate Rarity |
|---|---|
| 1–4 | Common, Uncommon |
| 5–10 | Uncommon, Rare |
| 11–16 | Rare, Very Rare |
| 17–20 | Very Rare, Legendary |

### Tips for Treasure

- **Tailor treasure to the party.** A +1 longsword is useless if no one uses longswords. Place items the party will enjoy.
- **Not everything is gold.** Trade goods, favors, property, and titles can be excellent rewards.
- **Magic items should feel special.** In 5e, magic items are meant to be rare. Give them history and personality.
- **Consider the economy.** A hoard of 10,000 gp can break a low-level game if there's nothing expensive to buy.
- **Art objects and gems** are great because they have clear value but aren't just coins.

title: NPC Creation
summary: How to create memorable NPCs with personality traits, motivations, secrets, and stat blocks — from quick improv to detailed preparation.
content:
## NPC Creation

NPCs bring the world to life. A well-crafted NPC can become a beloved ally, a feared rival, or a memorable one-scene character. The key is giving each NPC at least one distinctive trait and a clear motivation.

### Quick NPC Formula

For NPCs you create on the fly, you need three things:

1. **One distinctive trait** (voice, mannerism, appearance, or attitude)
2. **One motivation** (what do they want right now?)
3. **One connection** (how do they relate to the current situation?)

That's it. You can run an entire session with NPCs built this way.

### Detailed NPC Creation

For important recurring NPCs, flesh out these elements:

#### Appearance
- One or two distinctive physical features (not just race/gender).
- How they dress and what that says about them.
- Body language and how they carry themselves.

#### Personality Traits (d8)

| d8 | Trait |
|---|---|
| 1 | Speaks in a whisper, leans in close |
| 2 | Laughs nervously after every sentence |
| 3 | Constantly fidgets with a small object (coin, ring, etc.) |
| 4 | Overly formal, even in casual situations |
| 5 | Interrupts others to tell a "related" story |
| 6 | Avoids eye contact, stares at the ground |
| 7 | Uses elaborate hand gestures |
| 8 | Speaks in metaphors or riddles |

#### Motivations

Every NPC wants something. The more specific, the better:

| d10 | Motivation |
|---|---|
| 1 | Protect someone they love |
| 2 | Pay off a debt |
| 3 | Gain revenge on a specific person |
| 4 | Earn enough gold to retire |
| 5 | Prove themselves worthy of something |
| 6 | Hide a shameful secret |
| 7 | Find a missing person or object |
| 8 | Gain power or status |
| 9 | Atone for a past wrong |
| 10 | Satisfy an obsessive curiosity |

#### Secrets

Every important NPC should have at least one secret:

| d8 | Secret |
|---|---|
| 1 | They're not who they claim to be |
| 2 | They work for the antagonist (willingly or under duress) |
| 3 | They committed a crime that was blamed on someone else |
| 4 | They possess a hidden magical ability or item |
| 5 | They know a crucial piece of information they're afraid to share |
| 6 | They're dying of a disease or curse |
| 7 | They have a second family or life no one knows about |
| 8 | They are the last survivor of a destroyed group |

#### Bonds

- Who do they care about? (family, friend, mentor, rival)
- Who do they owe? (patron, crime boss, deity)
- Who do they fear? (enemy, authority figure, supernatural being)

### Using Published NPC Stat Blocks

The Monster Manual and other books contain generic NPC stat blocks. Use these as templates:

| Stat Block | CR | Use For |
|---|---|---|
| Commoner | 0 | Villagers, merchants, bystanders |
| Guard | 1/8 | Town guards, sentries, hired muscle |
| Bandit | 1/8 | Thugs, pirates, outlaws |
| Bandit Captain | 2 | Gang leaders, mercenary captains |
| Noble | 1/8 | Aristocrats, merchants, officials |
| Spy | 1 | Information brokers, infiltrators |
| Berserker | 2 | Barbarian warriors, bodyguards |
| Knight | 3 | Paladins, honor guards, champions |
| Veteran | 3 | Experienced soldiers, retired adventurers |
| Mage | 6 | Court wizards, tower mages |
| Assassin | 8 | Contract killers, elite agents |
| Archmage | 12 | Powerful wizards, guild leaders |

### Modifying Stat Blocks

Don't build NPCs from scratch — modify existing stat blocks:
- Swap spells to fit the NPC's theme.
- Add or remove one or two abilities.
- Change damage types (a fire cultist's scimitar deals fire damage).
- Adjust HP up or down by 20–30% to fine-tune difficulty.
- Give them a distinctive combat tactic that reflects their personality.

### Voice and Roleplaying Tips

- **You don't need accents.** Changing your cadence, volume, or word choice is enough.
- **Speak in character briefly.** A sentence or two in character, then describe in third person.
- **Write down NPC names and traits.** You will forget otherwise.
- **Reuse NPCs.** A shopkeeper who appeared once can become a recurring contact.
- **Let players name NPCs.** If you blank on a name, ask "what do the locals call this tavern keeper?" Players love this.

title: World Building Tips
summary: Practical advice for building a campaign world — starting small, building out, session zero preparation, and establishing tone and expectations.
content:
## World Building Tips

You don't need to create a whole world before your first session. Start small, build outward, and let player actions shape the world.

### Start Small

Begin with what the players will actually interact with in the first few sessions:

1. **One settlement** (village, town, or city district).
2. **One nearby adventure site** (dungeon, ruin, cave, haunted house).
3. **Three NPCs** the players will definitely meet (quest giver, ally, rival/obstacle).
4. **One regional threat** (what's the danger that motivates adventure?).
5. **A vague sense** of what's beyond — neighboring kingdoms, distant threats — without details.

### Build Outward

After the first few sessions, expand based on where the players want to go:

- **The players headed north?** Now you need to detail the northern region.
- **They asked about the king?** Now you need a political structure.
- **They want to join a guild?** Now you need guild details.

This "just in time" approach prevents wasted prep and ensures the world grows in directions the players care about.

### The Three-Layer Approach

**Layer 1 — The Local (detailed):** The town, its NPCs, its immediate surroundings. Know this well.

**Layer 2 — The Regional (sketched):** The surrounding area, major factions, nearby dungeons, travel routes. Broad strokes.

**Layer 3 — The Global (vague):** Empires, gods, history, distant lands. One-sentence descriptions. Fill in later.

### Session Zero

Before your first game session, hold a "Session Zero" to establish expectations:

**Must Discuss:**
- **Tone and genre.** Is this a lighthearted romp or a gritty survival game? Horror? Political intrigue? Heroic fantasy?
- **Content boundaries.** What topics are off-limits? Use safety tools (Lines and Veils, X-Card).
- **Character creation together.** Characters should have reasons to be together and care about each other.
- **House rules.** Critical fumbles? Flanking? Resurrection? Encumbrance? PvP?
- **Scheduling.** How often, how long, what happens when someone can't make it?
- **Communication.** How to give feedback, raise concerns, request changes.

**Good to Discuss:**
- What kind of adventures the players enjoy (combat, exploration, social, puzzle).
- Backstory integration — how much, and how will it tie into the campaign?
- Character death — how permanent is it? What are the stakes?
- Technology level and magic level of the setting.

### Tone Setting

Tone is the single most important worldbuilding decision. Everything else flows from it:

| Tone | Characteristics | Example Settings |
|---|---|---|
| Heroic Fantasy | Players are heroes, good vs. evil, epic stakes | Forgotten Realms, Dragonlance |
| Gritty/Low Fantasy | Dangerous world, moral ambiguity, scarce magic | Greyhawk, Dark Sun |
| Horror | Vulnerability, dread, unknown threats | Ravenloft |
| Noir/Intrigue | Mystery, deception, factions, urban | Eberron, Ravnica |
| Whimsical/Comedy | Lighthearted, absurd, fun over drama | Spelljammer |
| Mythic | Gods, destiny, legendary deeds | Theros |

### Naming Conventions

Consistent naming helps the world feel real:
- Pick a real-world language or culture as inspiration for each region (Norse names for the northern kingdom, Swahili-inspired names for the southern empire).
- Keep names **short and pronounceable**. "Zoranthariel" is hard to say; "Zora" works.
- **Write names down.** You will forget them.

### Common World Building Mistakes

- **Over-preparing.** Don't write a 50-page history document. Prep what you need for the next session.
- **The "novel" problem.** This is a collaborative story. Leave room for players to contribute.
- **Ignoring player backstories.** Weave character backgrounds into the world.
- **Too many factions/NPCs.** Players can track 3–5 factions and maybe 10 named NPCs. More than that causes confusion.
- **Lack of conflict.** Every location, faction, and NPC should have at least one source of tension or conflict.
- **The "perfect" world.** A perfect world is boring. Conflict, inequality, danger, and mystery drive adventure.

title: Running the Game
summary: Core DM techniques for running sessions — pacing, improvisation, the Rule of Cool, when to call for rolls, and how to keep the game moving.
content:
## Running the Game

Running D&D is a skill that improves with practice. These guidelines will help you run smoother, more engaging sessions.

### Pacing

Good pacing keeps players engaged. Bad pacing makes sessions drag or feel rushed.

**Signs of bad pacing:**
- Players are checking phones or side-talking.
- Combat rounds are taking 5+ minutes each.
- The party has spent 30 minutes debating a minor decision.
- Nothing has happened in the last 20 minutes of real time.

**How to fix pacing:**
- **Cut to the action.** Skip travel montages, shopping trips, and long rests unless something interesting happens during them.
- **Use a timer for decisions.** If the party can't decide in 2–3 minutes, have the world force a decision (guards arrive, the door starts closing, a monster attacks).
- **Alternate encounter types.** Don't run three combat encounters in a row. Alternate combat, social, exploration, and puzzle encounters.
- **End sessions on cliffhangers.** Stop at a dramatic moment to maintain energy between sessions.

### Improvisation

No matter how much you prep, players will do something unexpected. Improvisation is essential.

**The "Yes, And" Technique:**
- When a player tries something creative, say "yes, and..." — accept their action and add a complication or consequence.
- "I want to swing from the chandelier onto the ogre." → "Yes, and the chandelier starts to break loose from the ceiling. Make an Athletics check."

**The "Yes, But" Technique:**
- Accept the player's intent but add a cost or complication.
- "I want to persuade the guard to let us pass." → "Yes, but he wants something in return — he's heard you're adventurers and needs a favor."

**When to Say No:**
- When the action would break the game's internal logic.
- When it would ruin another player's fun.
- When it trivializes a challenge you want to matter.
- Even then, offer an alternative: "You can't do that, but you could try this instead."

### The Rule of Cool

If a player attempts something dramatic, creative, and awesome that doesn't quite fit the rules, lean toward allowing it. The memorable moments in D&D come from creative play, not strict rules adherence.

**Guidelines:**
- The Rule of Cool should require a roll — it shouldn't be free.
- It should happen occasionally, not every round. If it becomes routine, it's no longer cool.
- It shouldn't invalidate another player's class features or abilities.
- It should make everyone at the table excited.

### When to Call for Rolls

**Call for a roll when:**
- The outcome is uncertain AND failure has consequences.
- The task is neither trivially easy nor obviously impossible.
- The result would be interesting either way.

**Don't call for a roll when:**
- The task is so easy any competent person could do it (climbing a ladder, opening an unlocked door).
- The task is impossible (lifting a castle, persuading a fanatic to betray their faith with a single sentence).
- Failure would stop the adventure (if they must find the clue to proceed, just give it to them).
- The player's description is sufficient (a detailed, well-reasoned argument to an NPC who has reason to agree doesn't need a Persuasion check).

### Handling Rules Disputes

- **Make a ruling, move on.** Don't spend more than 30 seconds looking up a rule during play.
- **Note it down.** Look up the actual rule after the session and announce the correct ruling next time.
- **Be consistent.** Whatever you rule, apply it the same way in the future.
- **The DM's ruling is final at the table.** Players can discuss after the session.

### Managing Spotlight

Every player should get roughly equal time in the spotlight:

- **Ask quiet players directly.** "Kira, what is your character doing while this is happening?"
- **Create moments for each character.** A lock for the rogue, a religious question for the cleric, a feat of strength for the barbarian.
- **Rotate initiative order.** In social scenes, go around the table instead of letting the most vocal player dominate.
- **Split the party sometimes.** Brief 5-minute solo scenes make characters feel important.

### Combat Flow Tips

- **Roll attack and damage together.** Don't wait to see if you hit before rolling damage.
- **Use average damage for monsters.** The stat block lists it — use it to speed things up.
- **Describe, don't just declare.** "The orc swings its greataxe at your neck" is better than "the orc attacks you."
- **Prompt the next player.** "Aria, you're up. Bren, you're on deck."
- **Set a soft time limit.** If a player takes more than 60 seconds to decide, their character Dodges.

### The Most Important Rule

Everyone at the table — including the DM — should be having fun. If a rule, encounter, or situation is making the game less fun, change it. D&D is a game, and games exist to be enjoyed.
tags: dm-tool,running the game,pacing,improvisation,rule of cool,spotlight,combat flow,rules disputes,new dm

title: Ability Check DCs
summary: Difficulty Class guidelines for ability checks, from DC 5 (Very Easy) to DC 30 (Nearly Impossible), including when to use passive checks.
content:
## Ability Check DCs

The Difficulty Class (DC) of an ability check reflects how hard the task is. The DM sets the DC based on the challenge.

### Standard DCs

| DC | Difficulty | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | Very Easy | Noticing something obvious, climbing a knotted rope |
| 10 | Easy | Picking a simple lock, calming a spooked horse, cooking a decent meal |
| 15 | Medium | Picking a typical lock, navigating a forest, persuading a skeptical guard |
| 20 | Hard | Picking a high-quality lock, tracking a creature through rain, convincing a hostile NPC |
| 25 | Very Hard | Picking a masterwork lock, feats of extraordinary athleticism, recalling extremely obscure lore |
| 30 | Nearly Impossible | Tasks at the edge of mortal ability — only achievable with high bonuses and/or magical assistance |

### How to Set DCs

**Default to 15.** If you're unsure, DC 15 is a reasonable default for any moderately challenging task. Most DMs find that DCs of 10, 15, and 20 cover the vast majority of situations.

**Consider the fiction, not the player's bonus.** The DC should reflect the difficulty of the task itself, not whether the specific character will succeed. A DC 20 climb is hard for everyone, whether the character has +2 or +10 to Athletics.

**Adjust for circumstances:**
- Favorable conditions: Lower the DC by 2–5, or grant advantage.
- Unfavorable conditions: Raise the DC by 2–5, or impose disadvantage.
- Generally prefer advantage/disadvantage over DC adjustments.

### DC Probability Guide

This shows the approximate chance of success based on total modifier:

| Total Modifier | DC 10 | DC 15 | DC 20 | DC 25 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| +0 | 55% | 30% | 5% | — |
| +3 | 70% | 45% | 20% | — |
| +5 | 80% | 55% | 30% | 5% |
| +7 | 90% | 65% | 40% | 15% |
| +10 | 95% | 80% | 55% | 30% |
| +13 | 95% | 90% | 70% | 45% |

*("—" means impossible without advantage, Inspiration, or similar bonuses.)*

### Passive Checks

A passive check is used when the DM doesn't want to call for a roll — typically for things characters do constantly without actively trying.

**Passive Check = 10 + all applicable modifiers**

If the character has advantage, add 5. If disadvantage, subtract 5.

**Common Passive Checks:**
- **Passive Perception:** The most commonly used passive check. Determines whether characters notice hidden creatures, traps, or secret doors without actively searching. Compare the passive score to the DC (Stealth check of hiding creature, trap DC, etc.).
- **Passive Investigation:** Some traps or illusions use passive Investigation instead of passive Perception.
- **Passive Insight:** Can determine whether an NPC is lying without the player asking to roll.

**When to Use Passive vs. Active:**
- **Passive:** When the character isn't actively trying — walking down a hallway, having a conversation, traveling.
- **Active (roll):** When the character specifically declares they are searching, investigating, or concentrating on something.
- **General rule:** Passive checks represent baseline awareness. Active checks represent focused effort.

### Group Checks

When the whole party attempts something together (sneaking past guards, climbing a cliff), call for a group check:

1. Everyone rolls the same check.
2. If at **least half** the group succeeds, the whole group succeeds.
3. Otherwise, the group fails.

Group checks prevent one bad roll from ruining stealth or survival for the whole party.

### Contests

When two creatures directly oppose each other, use a contest instead of a DC:

- Both sides roll the relevant ability check.
- The higher result wins.
- Ties go to the defender (the one maintaining the status quo).

Examples: arm wrestling (STR vs. STR), hiding vs. searching (Stealth vs. Perception), grappling (Athletics vs. Athletics or Acrobatics).

### Common Mistakes

- **Calling for too many rolls.** If the outcome isn't in doubt, don't roll. If the barbarian wants to break down a flimsy wooden door, just let them.
- **Setting DCs too high.** DC 25+ should be extremely rare. Most tasks should be DC 10–20.
- **Ignoring passive scores.** If a character has a passive Perception of 19, they should notice most hidden things without rolling.
- **Not using contests.** When two characters oppose each other, use a contest, not a static DC.

title: Object AC and HP
summary: Armor Class and hit points for objects by material and size, including damage thresholds, immunities, and guidelines for destroying objects in play.
content:
## Object AC and HP

Sometimes characters want to smash a door, cut a rope, shatter a window, or destroy an item. These rules cover how objects take damage.

### Object Armor Class

An object's AC reflects how hard it is to deal meaningful damage to it.

| Material | AC |
|---|---|
| Cloth, paper, rope | 11 |
| Crystal, glass, ice | 13 |
| Wood, bone | 15 |
| Stone | 17 |
| Iron, steel | 19 |
| Mithral | 21 |
| Adamantine | 23 |

### Object Hit Points

An object's hit points reflect its size and structural integrity. The DM determines HP based on size and resilience.

| Size | Fragile | Resilient |
|---|---|---|
| Tiny (bottle, lock, vial) | 2 (1d4) | 5 (2d4) |
| Small (chest, lute, shield) | 3 (1d6) | 10 (3d6) |
| Medium (barrel, chandelier, door) | 4 (1d8) | 18 (4d8) |
| Large (cart, wagon, 10-ft-square window) | 5 (1d10) | 27 (5d10) |
| Huge (section of wall, wide gate) | — | 36+ |
| Gargantuan (castle wall, tower) | — | 50+ |

**Fragile** objects are made of thin, breakable materials (glass, crystal, ceramic). **Resilient** objects are solid and sturdy (wood, stone, iron).

### Damage Thresholds

Some large, solid objects have a **damage threshold** — they ignore damage below a certain amount from a single hit.

| Object | Damage Threshold |
|---|---|
| Canvas or rope | — |
| Wooden door | — |
| Reinforced wooden door | 5 |
| Iron door | 10 |
| Stone wall (1 ft thick) | 10 |
| Castle wall | 15 |
| Adamantine object | 20 |

If an attack deals less than the threshold, it deals no damage. If it exceeds the threshold, it deals full damage (not reduced by the threshold).

### Common Object Statistics

Here are ready-to-use stats for objects characters frequently interact with:

| Object | AC | HP | Damage Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple wooden door | 15 | 10 | — |
| Reinforced door | 15 | 18 | 5 |
| Iron door | 19 | 27 | 10 |
| Locked chest (wood) | 15 | 10 | — |
| Iron chest | 19 | 18 | 5 |
| Rope (1 inch thick) | 11 | 4 | — |
| Chain | 19 | 10 | — |
| Manacles | 19 | 10 | — |
| Glass window | 13 | 2 | — |
| Mirror | 13 | 3 | — |
| Wooden barrel | 15 | 8 | — |
| Stone statue (Medium) | 17 | 18 | 5 |
| Wooden bridge (section) | 15 | 27 | 5 |
| Brick wall (1 ft thick, 10 ft section) | 17 | 36 | 10 |
| Stone wall (1 ft thick, 10 ft section) | 17 | 45 | 10 |
| Crystal ball | 13 | 3 | — |
| Rowboat | 15 | 50 | — |
| Wagon | 15 | 27 | 5 |
| Potion vial | 13 | 2 | — |

### Object Immunities

All objects are immune to:
- **Poison damage**
- **Psychic damage**

Most objects are also immune to conditions (charmed, frightened, etc.), though magical objects may have exceptions.

Some objects may have additional resistances or immunities:
- A fire-resistant chest is immune to fire damage.
- A stone wall is resistant to slashing and piercing damage.

### Siege Weapons and Structures

Some attacks have the **siege** property, meaning they deal double damage to objects and structures. This is relevant for:
- Battering rams
- Trebuchets and catapults
- Some monster attacks (giants, dragons)

### Practical Rulings

- **Smashing a potion:** If an enemy is holding a potion, you can target it (AC 13, 2 HP) — but you need to beat the enemy's AC to hit them first, or use a called shot (DM ruling, usually at disadvantage).
- **Breaking manacles:** A creature restrained in manacles can try to break them with a DC 20 Strength check, or an ally can attack them (AC 19, HP 10).
- **Destroying a door:** Rather than tracking HP, some DMs simply set a Strength DC: 15 for a wooden door, 25 for a reinforced door, 28+ for an iron door.
- **Worn/carried objects:** Generally, worn or carried objects are protected by the creature holding them and can't be targeted separately unless the DM allows called shots.
